metadata_information.title: Paid Maternity Leave and Female Employment : Evidence Using Firm-Level Survey Data for Developing Countries
metadata_information.version: Python script, from D&R API, generated on 2026-03-19

title_statement.title: Paid Maternity Leave and Female Employment : Evidence Using Firm-Level Survey Data for Developing Countries

document_description.abstract: The relationship between the length of paid maternity leave and the proportion of female workers in the private sector is explored using firm-level survey data for 66 mostly developing countries. The paper finds a large, positive, and statistically significant relationship between the two. According to the most conservative estimate, an increase of one week of paid maternity leave is associated with a 2.6 percentage points increase in the share of workers in a typical firm that are female. As expected, the stated relationship is much larger when the government pays for maternity leave versus the employer. The results are robust to several controls for firm and country characteristics and other possible heterogeneities in the maternity leave and female workers relationship.

An objective of the series is to get the findings out quickly, even if the presentations are less than fully polished. The papers carry the names of the authors and should be cited accordingly.
